Editas Medicine Announces U.S. Patent and Trademark Office Reaffirms its Prior Decision in Favor of the Broad Institute in CRISPR/Cas9 Interference March 27, 2026 07:00 ET | Source: Editas Medicine, Inc. Follow Editas Medicine, Inc. Share CAMBRIDGE, Mass., March  27, 2026  (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Editas Medicine, Inc. (Nasdaq: EDIT), a pioneering gene editing company focused on developing transformative medicines for serious diseases, today announced that the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office reaffirmed the Patent Trial and Appeal Board’s (PTAB’s) previous decision favoring the Broad Institute in the U.S. patent interference involving specific patents for CRISPR/Cas9 editing in human cells between the University of California, the University of Vienna, and Emmanuelle Charpentier (collectively, CVC) and the Broad Institute, Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), and Harvard University (collectively, Broad). The patent interference was on remand from the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit (CAFC) following the CAFC’s May 2025 decision to affirm-in-part and vacate-in-part the PTAB’s prior decision. This action by the PTAB is its third favorable decision determining that Broad was the first to invent the use of CRISPR/Cas9 for gene editing in eukaryotic cells, including human cells. CVC retains the right to appeal the decision to the CAFC. “We are pleased with decision reaffirming Broad’s inventorship priority for CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing,” said Gilmore O’Neill, M.B., M.M.Sc., President and Chief Executive Officer, Editas Medicine. “This outcome reinforces our confidence in our intellectual property as we continue to leverage the power of in vivo gene editing to create transformative medicines for people living with serious diseases. This includes the development of EDIT-401, an experimental, potential best-in-class, one-time therapy that has achieved an unprecedented greater than 90 percent mean LDL cholesterol reduction in non-human primates.” The CRISPR/Cas9 patents at issue are exclusively licensed to Editas Medicine for the development and commercialization of CRISPR/Cas9-based medicines. Other in-licensed patents from the Broad Institute, Harvard University, MIT, and other institutions covering CRISPR/Cas9, as well as those in-licensed patents from the Broad Institute and collaborators covering CRISPR/Cas12a, are not at issue in the interference and are unaffected by this decision. Editas Medicine’s foundational intellectual property includes issued patents covering fundamental aspects of both CRISPR/Cas12a and CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing in all human cells. Additionally, the Company holds a wide range of fundamental intellectual property directed to all the components of its gene editing platform including product-enabling and product-specific intellectual property covering the use of CRISPR/Cas12a and CRISPR/Cas9 for gene editing of human cells in the United States, Australia, Europe, Japan, China, and other jurisdictions. About Editas Medicine As a pioneering gene editing company, Editas Medicine is focused on translating the power and potential of CRISPR genome editing systems into a robust pipeline of in vivo medicines for people living with serious diseases around the world. Editas Medicine aims to discover, develop, manufacture, and commercialize transformative, durable, precision in vivo gene editing medicines for a broad class of diseases. Editas Medicine is the exclusive licensee of Broad Institute’s Cas12a patent estate and Broad Institute and Harvard University’s Cas9 patent estates for human medicines. For the latest information and scientific presentations, please visit www.editasmedicine.com . articles.title: Intellia Announces Proposed Public Offering of Common Stock
articles.text:
Accessibility: Skip TopNav Intellia Therapeutics Reports Inducement Grants Under Nasdaq Listing Rule 5635(c)(4) Intellia Therapeutics Reports Inducement Grants Under Nasdaq Listing Rule 5635(c)(4) May 01, 2026 16:01 ET | Source: Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. Follow Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. Share CAMBRIDGE, Mass., May  01, 2026  (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (Nasdaq: NTLA), a leading biopharmaceutical company focused on revolutionizing medicine leveraging CRISPR gene editing and other core technologies, today announced that on May 1, 2026, it awarded inducement grants to 43 new employees under Intellia’s 2024 Inducement Plan, as amended, as a material inducement to employment. The inducement grants consisted of time-based restricted stock units (“RSUs”) for an aggregate of 208,850 shares of Intellia’s common stock, with one-third of such RSUs vesting annually over three years. All equity vesting is subject to each employee’s continued service as an employee of, or other service provider to, Intellia through the applicable vesting dates. All of the above-described awards were granted outside of Intellia’s stockholder-approved equity incentive plans pursuant to Intellia’s 2024 Inducement Plan, as amended, which was initially adopted by the board of directors in June 2024. These awards were approved by Intellia’s compensation committee as a material inducement to entering into employment with Intellia in accordance with Nasdaq Listing Rule 5635(c)(4). About Intellia Therapeutics Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (Nasdaq: NTLA) is a leading clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focused on revolutionizing medicine leveraging CRISPR gene editing and other core technologies. The company’s mission is to transform the lives of people with severe diseases by developing and commercializing potentially curative treatments. With deep scientific, technical and clinical development experience, Intellia aims to reset the standard for medicine by durably treating the root causes of disease. Learn more at intelliatx.com and follow us @intelliatx . Investor Contact: Jason Fredette Vice President, Investor Relations and Corporate Communications Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. jason.fredette@intelliatx.com Media Contact: Mike Tattory Vice President LifeSci Communications mtattory@lifescicommunications.com GlobeNewswire Company Profile Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. The inducement grants consisted of time-based restricted stock units (“RSUs”) for an aggregate of 208,850 shares of Intellia’s common stock, with one-third of such RSUs vesting annually over three years. All equity vesting is subject to each employee’s continued service as an employee of, or other service provider to, Intellia through the applicable vesting dates. All of the above-described awards were granted outside of Intellia’s stockholder-approved equity incentive plans pursuant to Intellia’s 2024 Inducement Plan, as amended, which was initially adopted by the board of directors in June 2024. These awards were approved by Intellia’s compensation committee as a material inducement to entering into employment with Intellia in accordance with Nasdaq Listing Rule 5635(c)(4). About Intellia Therapeutics Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (Nasdaq: NTLA) is a leading clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focused on revolutionizing medicine leveraging CRISPR gene editing and other core technologies. The company’s mission is to transform the lives of people with severe diseases by developing and commercializing potentially curative treatments. With deep scientific, technical and clinical development experience, Intellia aims to reset the standard for medicine by durably treating the root causes of disease. Learn more at intelliatx.com and follow us @intelliatx . Advanced gene editing ‘promising’ for sickle cell disease, but access challenges remain

Back to Healio Save Share LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) Bluesky Threads Email Copy Link Key takeaways: Two studies highlight the potential of gene editing for sickle cell disease treatment. Despite increasing evidence of efficacy, high costs and manufacturing challenges have limited access to gene therapy. Two papers published in The New England Journal of Medicine highlight the potential of gene editing for treatment of sickle cell disease. In one study, renizgamglogene autogedtemcel (Editas Medicine) led to normalization of total hemoglobin levels and a higher level of fetal hemoglobin. In the other investigation, ristoglogene autogetemcel (Beam Therapeutics) resulted in rapid engraftment and durable expression of fetal hemoglobin. “I truly think what we are seeing truly amounts to a functional cure,” Rabi Hanna, MD, chairman of the division of pediatric hematology/oncology and bone marrow transplantation at Cleveland Clinic Children’s and an investigator on both studies, told Healio. Gene therapy ‘2.0’ Approximately 100,000 Americans have sickle cell disease, according to CDC statistics. More than 90% of them are non-Hispanic Black or African American. Disease-modifying therapies can alleviate symptoms — such as painful vaso-occlusive events and chronic hemolytic anemia — but they do not address the underlying cause of the condition, which can lead to multiorgan damage and death. Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation can be curative but its use is limited by a lack of HLA-matched donors, as well as risks for graft-versus-host disease. Autologous HSCT gene therapies can overcome some of the limitations associated with allogeneic transplant. The FDA has approved two such therapies for people with sickle cell disease aged 12 years or older with recurrent vaso-occlusive crises. Both use a patient’s own hematopoietic stem cells. With exagamglogene autotemcel (Casgevy; Vertex Pharmaceuticals/CRISPR Therapeutics), CRISPR-Cas9 is used to edit the BCL11A gene, with modifications designed to produce fetal hemoglobin. With lovotibeglogene autotemcel (Lyfgenia, Genetix Biotherapeutics), a lentiviral vector is used to insert a functional, modified beta-globin gene into the stem cells. “The whole community that takes care of people with sickle cell disease has been excited about these transformative gene therapies," Hanna said. “However, in real-world settings, we do see challenges related to access, peripheral blood stem cell mobilization and collection, and manufacturing — whether using gene addition or gene editing. Improving these aspects will allow us to reach more patients effectively. This isn’t to take away from the great innovation and what we have available, but I call that gene therapy version 1. We need to move to version 2.0.” RUBY study Renizgamglogene autogedtemcel, often called reni-cel, is a CRISPR-Cas12a gene-edited autologous hematopoietic stem cell therapy. Editing of CD34-positive cells at the BCL11A transcriptional repressor binding site in the HBG1 / HBG2 promoters is performed to reactivate fetal hemoglobin expression. “Patients who have a mutation in [ HBG1 or HBG2 ] have a higher level of fetal hemoglobin,” Hanna said. “That tends to be protective because they don’t really have a severe phenotype. With reni-cel, the gene editing is trying to mimic what happens in nature.” Cas12a typically offers greater gene-editing specificity than Cas9, with the goal to decrease off-target effects, Hanna said. Hanna and colleagues conducted the phase 1/phase2 RUBY study to evaluate whether treatment with reni-cel could offer durable benefit to people with severe sickle cell disease. The multicenter, open-label study included patients aged 12 to 50 years who had experienced at least two severe vaso-occlusive events in each of the prior 2 years. Patients underwent myeloablative conditioning with busulfan, followed by a single reni-cel infusion. Researchers followed patients for 24 months to assess engraftment, hemoglobin-related measures, vaso-occlusive events, adverse events and allelic editing levels. Investigators performed a nonprespecified analysis of efficacy and safety data from the first 28 adults who received treatment. Median follow-up reached 9.5 months (range, 0.7-25.2). Twenty-seven patients had neutrophil engraftment — occurring after a median 23 days (range, 14-29) — and platelet engraftment, occurring after a median 25 days (range, 17-51). Mean percentage of fetal hemoglobin — which reduces the likelihood that red blood cells will stiffen into a sickle shape and clog blood vessels — increased from 2.5% at baseline to 48.1% at 6 months. Mean total hemoglobin — which minimizes sickle cell disease-related complications — increased from 9.8 g/dL at baseline to 13.8 g/dL at 6 months, a level within the normal range for people who do not have sickle cell disease Fetal and total hemoglobin levels have remained steady over time, Hanna said, with median follow-up now reaching 17 months and follow-up for some patients exceeding 2 years. “We saw promising efficacy,” Hanna said. “The number of stem cells produced was very good, as was engraftment. We are seeing red blood cells as truly functionally normal cells. “We showed there is no pain, but we need more data to know whether there is improvement in end-organ damage,” Hanna added. “Hopefully in 5 or 10 years, we will be able to say this prolonged patients’ lives and gave them good quality of life.” Adverse events appeared consistent with those typically reported after myeloablative conditioning with busulfan or autologous HSCT, according to investigators. Twenty-seven of 28 patients had no vaso-occlusive events. One patient experienced two severe vaso-occlusive events after infusion. Editas Medicine discontinued reni-cel development in December 2024 as part of a companywide reassessment of clinical priorities — which resulted in greater focus on in vivo gene editing — and the company’s decision prompted early termination of the RUBY study. Still, long-term follow-up of patients enrolled in Ruby will continue. BEACON study Ristoglogene autogetemcel, often called risto-cel, is an autologous hematopoietic stem cell therapy developed using base editing technology that avoids viral vectors. CD34-positive hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells are base edited to target HBG1 / HBG2 to increase fetal hemoglobin production. The precise single-strand gene editing approach allows for DNA modification without the introduction of double-strand breaks. In the phase 1/phase 2 BEACON study, researchers evaluated risto-cel for people aged 12 to 35 years with sickle cell disease who had a minimum four severe vaso-occlusive crises in the 2 years prior to enrollment. Thirty-one patients underwent myeloablative conditioning with busulfan, followed by one infusion of risto-cel. Freedom from severe vaso-occlusive crises for 12 consecutive months — beginning later than 60 days after the final red cell transfusion — served as the primary efficacy outcome. An unplanned interim analysis explored data related to the primary endpoint, as well as editing, engraftment, hemoglobin production and safety. Mean follow-up reached 6.6 months (range, 0.3-20.4). Neutrophil engraftment occurred by a median 17.5 days, with platelet engraftment occurring by a median 19 days. Researchers reported favorable outcomes at 6 months, with the mean fraction of on-target edited alleles in peripheral blood reaching 67.4%. The mean level of hemoglobin S — an inherited variant responsible for sickle cell disease — as a fraction of total hemoglobin was below 40%, with levels remaining steady with longer follow-up. Investigators reported no severe vaso-occlusive crises more than 60 days after the final red cell transfusion. All patients experienced at least one adverse event, with 27 (87%) experiencing a grade 3 or higher event and 12 (39%) experiencing a serious adverse event. One patient died due to idiopathic pneumonia syndrome. Importantly, most patients completed stem cell collection after one or two cycles. “The number of cycles required for other gene therapies is much higher — sometimes up to six cycles — so this is huge for our patients,” Hanna said. “Stem cell collection has been a challenge and it has prohibited many patients from getting access to stem cell-modified gene therapy. Some patients can’t have enough stem cells to be sent to the company. If we can produce the required number with far fewer cycles because of improved gene editing technology, that will advance the field.” ‘The ideal situation’ The findings from the RUBY and BEACON studies support the utility of gene therapy and offer broader proof of efficacy of the CRISPR platform, Hanna said. “It not only works in BCL11A , but it works in [ HBG1 and HBG2 ],” he said. “Gene therapy needs to continue to be refined. I would challenge regulatory authorities to think about how we can move away from potentially approving one medicine based on one indication and think about whether this can be approved as a platform.” Even if new approaches make gene therapy safer and less time intensive, challenges related to its use for sickle cell disease remain. High costs and limited accessibility top the list, Hanna said. Less intense conditioning therapy that potentially has fewer side effects than busulfan — especially related to risk for infertility — also is needed, he said. “As a health care system, we need a better model for regulatory approval that will enable innovation,” he said. “These therapies are quite expensive. If we have more innovation, we will have more competition. That would decrease the cost of these therapies and improve access.” Additional efforts to improve manufacturing are needed, Hanna said. He also called for further research to identify strategies to reduce the toxicity of conditioning therapy and determine if gene therapy can improve vasculopathy, the key driver of stroke and end-organ damage in sickle cell disease. “The ideal situation that we dream about is to have options for our patients,” Hanna said. “We want to make sure every patient who needs a curative option — whether it is allogeneic transplant or gene therapy — can get it.” For more information: Rabi Hanna, MD, can be reached at hannar2@ccf.org. Gene editing therapy shows success against severe sickle cell disease. https://newsroom.clevelandclinic.org/2026/04/01/gene-editing-therapy-shows-success-against-severe-sickle-cell-disease . Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation can be curative but its use is limited by a lack of HLA-matched donors, as well as risks for graft-versus-host disease. Autologous HSCT gene therapies can overcome some of the limitations associated with allogeneic transplant. The FDA has approved two such therapies for people with sickle cell disease aged 12 years or older with recurrent vaso-occlusive crises. Both use a patient’s own hematopoietic stem cells. With exagamglogene autotemcel (Casgevy; Vertex Pharmaceuticals/CRISPR Therapeutics), CRISPR-Cas9 is used to edit the BCL11A gene, with modifications designed to produce fetal hemoglobin. With lovotibeglogene autotemcel (Lyfgenia, Genetix Biotherapeutics), a lentiviral vector is used to insert a functional, modified beta-globin gene into the stem cells. “The whole community that takes care of people with sickle cell disease has been excited about these transformative gene therapies," Hanna said. “However, in real-world settings, we do see challenges related to access, peripheral blood stem cell mobilization and collection, and manufacturing — whether using gene addition or gene editing. Improving these aspects will allow us to reach more patients effectively. This isn’t to take away from the great innovation and what we have available, but I call that gene therapy version 1. We need to move to version 2.0.” RUBY study Renizgamglogene autogedtemcel, often called reni-cel, is a CRISPR-Cas12a gene-edited autologous hematopoietic stem cell therapy. Editing of CD34-positive cells at the BCL11A transcriptional repressor binding site in the HBG1 / HBG2 promoters is performed to reactivate fetal hemoglobin expression. “Patients who have a mutation in [ HBG1 or HBG2 ] have a higher level of fetal hemoglobin,” Hanna said. “That tends to be protective because they don’t really have a severe phenotype. With reni-cel, the gene editing is trying to mimic what happens in nature.” Cas12a typically offers greater gene-editing specificity than Cas9, with the goal to decrease off-target effects, Hanna said. Hanna and colleagues conducted the phase 1/phase2 RUBY study to evaluate whether treatment with reni-cel could offer durable benefit to people with severe sickle cell disease. The multicenter, open-label study included patients aged 12 to 50 years who had experienced at least two severe vaso-occlusive events in each of the prior 2 years. Patients underwent myeloablative conditioning with busulfan, followed by a single reni-cel infusion. Researchers followed patients for 24 months to assess engraftment, hemoglobin-related measures, vaso-occlusive events, adverse events and allelic editing levels. Investigators performed a nonprespecified analysis of efficacy and safety data from the first 28 adults who received treatment. Median follow-up reached 9.5 months (range, 0.7-25.2). Twenty-seven patients had neutrophil engraftment — occurring after a median 23 days (range, 14-29) — and platelet engraftment, occurring after a median 25 days (range, 17-51). Mean percentage of fetal hemoglobin — which reduces the likelihood that red blood cells will stiffen into a sickle shape and clog blood vessels — increased from 2.5% at baseline to 48.1% at 6 months. Mean total hemoglobin — which minimizes sickle cell disease-related complications — increased from 9.8 g/dL at baseline to 13.8 g/dL at 6 months, a level within the normal range for people who do not have sickle cell disease Fetal and total hemoglobin levels have remained steady over time, Hanna said, with median follow-up now reaching 17 months and follow-up for some patients exceeding 2 years. “We saw promising efficacy,” Hanna said. “The number of stem cells produced was very good, as was engraftment. We are seeing red blood cells as truly functionally normal cells. “We showed there is no pain, but we need more data to know whether there is improvement in end-organ damage,” Hanna added. “Hopefully in 5 or 10 years, we will be able to say this prolonged patients’ lives and gave them good quality of life.” Adverse events appeared consistent with those typically reported after myeloablative conditioning with busulfan or autologous HSCT, according to investigators. Twenty-seven of 28 patients had no vaso-occlusive events. One patient experienced two severe vaso-occlusive events after infusion. Editas Medicine discontinued reni-cel development in December 2024 as part of a companywide reassessment of clinical priorities — which resulted in greater focus on in vivo gene editing — and the company’s decision prompted early termination of the RUBY study. Still, long-term follow-up of patients enrolled in Ruby will continue. BEACON study Ristoglogene autogetemcel, often called risto-cel, is an autologous hematopoietic stem cell therapy developed using base editing technology that avoids viral vectors. CD34-positive hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells are base edited to target HBG1 / HBG2 to increase fetal hemoglobin production. The precise single-strand gene editing approach allows for DNA modification without the introduction of double-strand breaks. In the phase 1/phase 2 BEACON study, researchers evaluated risto-cel for people aged 12 to 35 years with sickle cell disease who had a minimum four severe vaso-occlusive crises in the 2 years prior to enrollment. Thirty-one patients underwent myeloablative conditioning with busulfan, followed by one infusion of risto-cel. Freedom from severe vaso-occlusive crises for 12 consecutive months — beginning later than 60 days after the final red cell transfusion — served as the primary efficacy outcome. An unplanned interim analysis explored data related to the primary endpoint, as well as editing, engraftment, hemoglobin production and safety. Mean follow-up reached 6.6 months (range, 0.3-20.4). Neutrophil engraftment occurred by a median 17.5 days, with platelet engraftment occurring by a median 19 days. Researchers reported favorable outcomes at 6 months, with the mean fraction of on-target edited alleles in peripheral blood reaching 67.4%. The mean level of hemoglobin S — an inherited variant responsible for sickle cell disease — as a fraction of total hemoglobin was below 40%, with levels remaining steady with longer follow-up. Investigators reported no severe vaso-occlusive crises more than 60 days after the final red cell transfusion. All patients experienced at least one adverse event, with 27 (87%) experiencing a grade 3 or higher event and 12 (39%) experiencing a serious adverse event. One patient died due to idiopathic pneumonia syndrome. Importantly, most patients completed stem cell collection after one or two cycles. “The number of cycles required for other gene therapies is much higher — sometimes up to six cycles — so this is huge for our patients,” Hanna said. “Stem cell collection has been a challenge and it has prohibited many patients from getting access to stem cell-modified gene therapy. Some patients can’t have enough stem cells to be sent to the company. If we can produce the required number with far fewer cycles because of improved gene editing technology, that will advance the field.” ‘The ideal situation’ The findings from the RUBY and BEACON studies support the utility of gene therapy and offer broader proof of efficacy of the CRISPR platform, Hanna said. “It not only works in BCL11A , but it works in [ HBG1 and HBG2 ],” he said. “Gene therapy needs to continue to be refined. I would challenge regulatory authorities to think about how we can move away from potentially approving one medicine based on one indication and think about whether this can be approved as a platform.” Even if new approaches make gene therapy safer and less time intensive, challenges related to its use for sickle cell disease remain. High costs and limited accessibility top the list, Hanna said. Less intense conditioning therapy that potentially has fewer side effects than busulfan — especially related to risk for infertility — also is needed, he said. “As a health care system, we need a better model for regulatory approval that will enable innovation,” he said. “These therapies are quite expensive. If we have more innovation, we will have more competition. That would decrease the cost of these therapies and improve access.” Additional efforts to improve manufacturing are needed, Hanna said. He also called for further research to identify strategies to reduce the toxicity of conditioning therapy and determine if gene therapy can improve vasculopathy, the key driver of stroke and end-organ damage in sickle cell disease. “The ideal situation that we dream about is to have options for our patients,” Hanna said. “We want to make sure every patient who needs a curative option — whether it is allogeneic transplant or gene therapy — can get it.” For more information: Rabi Hanna, MD, can be reached at hannar2@ccf.org. Ristoglogene autogetemcel, often called risto-cel, is an autologous hematopoietic stem cell therapy developed using base editing technology that avoids viral vectors.
CD34-positive hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells are base edited to target HBG1 / HBG2 to increase fetal hemoglobin production. The precise single-strand gene editing approach allows for DNA modification without the introduction of double-strand breaks.
In the phase 1/phase 2 BEACON study, researchers evaluated risto-cel for people aged 12 to 35 years with sickle cell disease who had a minimum four severe vaso-occlusive crises in the 2 years prior to enrollment.
Thirty-one patients underwent myeloablative conditioning with busulfan, followed by one infusion of risto-cel.
Freedom from severe vaso-occlusive crises for 12 consecutive months — beginning later than 60 days after the final red cell transfusion — served as the primary efficacy outcome.
An unplanned interim analysis explored data related to the primary endpoint, as well as editing, engraftment, hemoglobin production and safety.
Mean follow-up reached 6.6 months (range, 0.3-20.4). Neutrophil engraftment occurred by a median 17.5 days, with platelet engraftment occurring by a median 19 days.
Researchers reported favorable outcomes at 6 months, with the mean fraction of on-target edited alleles in peripheral blood reaching 67.4%. The mean level of hemoglobin S — an inherited variant responsible for sickle cell disease — as a fraction of total hemoglobin was below 40%, with levels remaining steady with longer follow-up.
Investigators reported no severe vaso-occlusive crises more than 60 days after the final red cell transfusion.
All patients experienced at least one adverse event, with 27 (87%) experiencing a grade 3 or higher event and 12 (39%) experiencing a serious adverse event. One patient died due to idiopathic pneumonia syndrome.
Importantly, most patients completed stem cell collection after one or two cycles.
“The number of cycles required for other gene therapies is much higher — sometimes up to six cycles — so this is huge for our patients,” Hanna said. “Stem cell collection has been a challenge and it has prohibited many patients from getting access to stem cell-modified gene therapy. Some patients can’t have enough stem cells to be sent to the company. If we can produce the required number with far fewer cycles because of improved gene editing technology, that will advance the field.”
‘The ideal situation’
The findings from the RUBY and BEACON studies support the utility of gene therapy and offer broader proof of efficacy of the CRISPR platform, Hanna said.
“It not only works in BCL11A , but it works in [ HBG1 and HBG2 ],” he said. “Gene therapy needs to continue to be refined. I would challenge regulatory authorities to think about how we can move away from potentially approving one medicine based on one indication and think about whether this can be approved as a platform.”
Even if new approaches make gene therapy safer and less time intensive, challenges related to its use for sickle cell disease remain. High costs and limited accessibility top the list, Hanna said. Less intense conditioning therapy that potentially has fewer side effects than busulfan — especially related to risk for infertility — also is needed, he said.
“As a health care system, we need a better model for regulatory approval that will enable innovation,” he said. “These therapies are quite expensive. If we have more innovation, we will have more competition. That would decrease the cost of these therapies and improve access.”
Additional efforts to improve manufacturing are needed, Hanna said. He also called for further research to identify strategies to reduce the toxicity of conditioning therapy and determine if gene therapy can improve vasculopathy, the key driver of stroke and end-organ damage in sickle cell disease.
“The ideal situation that we dream about is to have options for our patients,” Hanna said. “We want to make sure every patient who needs a curative option — whether it is allogeneic transplant or gene therapy — can get it.”
For more information:
Rabi Hanna, MD, can be reached at hannar2@ccf.org.

articles.title: OxyContin maker Purdue Pharma set to dissolve after judge approves its criminal sentence
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Health OxyContin maker Purdue Pharma set to dissolve after judge approves its criminal sentence ‘It is not lost on me that those who started the epidemic will not serve a sentence,’ the judge said Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Kathleen Scarpone, left, and others protest in front of the Arthur M. Sackler Museum at Harvard University in Cambridge, Mass., in 2019. Scarpone lost her son to OxyContin addiction. Josh Reynolds/AP By Associated Press April 30, 2026 NEWARK, N.J. — OxyContin maker Purdue Pharma is set to be dissolved and replaced by a company focused on the public good by the week’s end, as a massive legal settlement resolving thousands of lawsuits takes effect. A federal judge on Tuesday delivered a criminal sentence to the company to resolve a Department of Justice probe — a last necessary step to clear the way for the settlement. Advertisement U.S. District Judge Madeline Cox Arleo made her decision after listening to hours of impact statements from people who lost loved ones or struggled with addiction themselves and requested she reject the negotiated sentence. While she didn’t go that far, she said she sympathized with people who bore the brunt of an epidemic linked to more than 900,000 deaths in the U.S. since 1999. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: In her own words: Surgeon general nominee Nicole Saphier expresses enthusiasm and caution for MAHA
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Health In her own words: Surgeon general nominee Nicole Saphier expresses enthusiasm and caution for MAHA ‘Questioning the vaccine schedule, that doesn’t mean you’re anti-vax’ Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Nicole Saphier, now the nominee for surgeon general, attends FOX Nation’s Patriot Awards in 2024. Theo Wargo/Getty Images By Elizabeth Cooney , Helen Branswell ,  and Katie Palmer April 30, 2026 Elizabeth Cooney elizabeth.cooney@statnews.com Elizabeth Cooney is a cardiovascular disease reporter at STAT, covering heart, stroke, and metabolic conditions. You can reach Liz on Signal at LizC.22. Get email alerts for Elizabeth Cooney Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Helen Branswell Helen Branswell covers issues broadly related to infectious diseases, including outbreaks, preparedness, research, and vaccine development. Follow her on Mastodon and Bluesky . You can reach Helen on Signal at hbranswell.01. Get email alerts for Helen Branswell Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Katie Palmer katie.palmer@statnews.com Katie Palmer covers telehealth, clinical artificial intelligence, and the health data economy — with an emphasis on the impacts of digital health care for patients, providers, and businesses. You can reach Katie on Signal at palmer.01. Get email alerts for Katie Palmer Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Now that Casey Means is no longer the Trump administration’s choice for surgeon general, attention is turning to the new nominee for the position. Nicole Saphier, whose candidacy was announced Thursday, is a licensed physician — unlike Means, whose license lapsed. A radiologist at the Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Saphier (pronounced SAA-fire) is director of breast imaging at MSK Monmouth in New Jersey. She may be more widely known as a regular contributor to Fox Business, where she has said that the overwhelming majority of “good research” disputes the notion that vaccines are linked to autism, but has expressed an openness to alternative childhood vaccine schedules. Advertisement Saphier has weighed in on many other concerns shared by the Make America Healthy Again movement promoted by health secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., agreeing with Kennedy on some positions but also clearly questioning others. In her own words, here are her views on vaccines, peptides, Tylenol in pregnancy, dietary guidelines, breast cancer, and also, Casey Means. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: Are international medical graduates taking residency spots from Americans? We did the math
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion Are international medical graduates taking residency spots from Americans? We did the math The data don’t support the narrative taking hold on social media Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Manal Khan , Abishek Bala ,  and Sarah Mohiuddin April 30, 2026 Khan is associate program director for the child/adolescent psychiatry fellowship at University of California, Los Angeles. Bala is associate program director of the child/adolescent psychiatry fellowship at Central Michigan University. Mohiuddin serves as the training director of the child and adolescent psychiatry fellowship and as chair of graduate medical education resident well‑being at Michigan Medicine. The 2026 Main Residency Match was the largest in the program’s 74-year history. With 48,050 active applicants, 41,482 residency training positions were filled . Among the celebrations, however, Match Day announcements triggered a surge of posts on X and other social media platforms calling for new restrictions on international medical graduates (IMGs) in the match, claiming that U.S. medical graduates (USMGs) had been displaced. These calls came from a range of voices: anti-immigration commentators and political figures with no medical background as well as physicians channeling workforce frustration into criticism of non-U.S. IMGs. Critics found headshots of incoming residents, posted by programs to celebrate their matches, and recirculated without consent to fuel this narrative. Certain policymakers and social media figures with large followings have amplified these claims and are actively pursuing legislation to restrict visa sponsorship for internationally trained physicians. Advertisement With the broader rise in anti-immigrant rhetoric, such claims warrant systematic examination. We reviewed the 2026 National Resident Matching Program (NRMP) data and found no evidentiary basis for the displacement hypothesis. Enacting policies on its premise would deepen the physician workforce crisis it purports to address. education , Physicians , public health Submit a correction request Reprints Manal Khan Abishek Bala Sarah Mohiuddin Newsletter The smartest thinkers in life sciences on what's happening — and what's to come Advertisement Most Popular Remembering J. Critics found headshots of incoming residents, posted by programs to celebrate their matches, and recirculated without consent to fuel this narrative. Certain policymakers and social media figures with large followings have amplified these claims and are actively pursuing legislation to restrict visa sponsorship for internationally trained physicians. Advertisement With the broader rise in anti-immigrant rhetoric, such claims warrant systematic examination. We reviewed the 2026 National Resident Matching Program (NRMP) data and found no evidentiary basis for the displacement hypothesis. Enacting policies on its premise would deepen the physician workforce crisis it purports to address. education , Physicians , public health Submit a correction request Reprints Manal Khan Abishek Bala Sarah Mohiuddin Newsletter The smartest thinkers in life sciences on what's happening — and what's to come Advertisement Most Popular Remembering J. articles.title: Health system CEOs get off easy at Congressional hearing on affordability
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Politics Health system CEOs get off easy at Congressional hearing on affordability Committee members seemed more interested in sniping at each other Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article New York-Presbyterian was one of four health systems represented at a hearing on hospitals and affordability on Tuesday. John Lamparski/Getty Images By Tara Bannow April 28, 2026 Hospitals and Insurance Reporter Tara Bannow tara.bannow@statnews.com Tara Bannow covers hospitals, providers, and insurers. You can reach Tara on Signal at tarabannow.70. Get email alerts for Tara Bannow Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts The four health system CEOs summoned before a Congressional committee Tuesday likely breathed sighs of relief early in the hearing, when it became clear they had friends in the audience. Instead, committee members largely blamed the other party’s health care policies for driving U.S. health care prices to levels inaccessible to many Americans. Advertisement The hearing was part of the House Ways and Means Committee’s effort to understand the root causes of rising health care costs in the U.S. It comes three months after the committee heard from the CEOs of the country’s largest health insurers, who largely deflected blame onto hospitals and drugmakers. In attendance were the CEOs of some of the country’s largest health systems: HCA Healthcare, a for-profit system of 190 hospitals, and CommonSpirit Health, a nonprofit system of 158 hospitals. The CEOs of New York-Presbyterian and North Carolina’s ECU Health were also there. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. three months after
In attendance were the CEOs of some of the country’s largest health systems: HCA Healthcare, a for-profit system of 190 hospitals, and CommonSpirit Health, a nonprofit system of 158 hospitals. The CEOs of New York-Presbyterian and North Carolina’s ECU Health were also there.

You can reach Theresa on Signal at theresagaff.97. Sign up here . Good morning. The other night I watched a shocking episode of “The Vampire Diaries.” A series of cursed, ghost-like hallucinations attempt to convince a teen vampire to end her own life using some disturbingly coercive, cogent arguments. Ultimately, the character is saved. And while this episode aired more than a decade ago, I was surprised by how many parallels there were to current debates about the risks of AI chatbots and people in mental health crises. articles.title: Eli Lilly enlists AI startup for next-generation gene editors
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Pharma Eli Lilly enlists AI startup for next-generation gene editors Flush with obesity profits, Lilly is making a big push into a hot topic Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Scott Olson/Getty Images By Jason Mast April 28, 2026 General Assignment Reporter Jason Mast jason.mast@statnews.com Jason Mast is a general assignment reporter at STAT focused on the science behind new medicines and the systems and people that decide whether that science ever reaches patients. You can reach Jason on Signal at JasonMast.05. Get email alerts for Jason Mast Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Eli Lilly struck a deal Tuesday to develop new forms of gene editors potentially capable of inserting entire genes into patients. The collaboration, with artificial intelligence-focused biotech Profluent, is sparse on details, including the number of programs the two companies would work on, the types of diseases they’ll pursue, or how much Lilly was paying upfront. But if every one of its efforts works out, Lilly would pay Profluent $2.25 billion in milestones payments. Advertisement The deal is part of a larger push by Lilly into gene editing. The big pharma, flush with record revenues from its obesity and diabetes drugs, has opened a new genetic medicine center in Boston and bought up a series of gene editing or gene therapy companies over the last few years. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: Google clinical director says AI can be a ‘bridge' for people having a mental health crisis
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Health tech Google clinical director says AI can be a ‘bridge’ for people having a mental health crisis Cutting users off ‘could do more harm than good,’ Megan Jones Bell told STAT Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Google clinical director Megan Jones Bell. Google By Mario Aguilar April 28, 2026 Health Tech Correspondent Mario Aguilar mario.aguilar@statnews.com Mario Aguilar covers technology in health care, including artificial intelligence, virtual reality, wearable devices, telehealth, and digital therapeutics. His stories explore how tech is changing the practice of health care and the business and policy challenges to realizing tech’s promise. He’s also the co-author of the free, twice weekly STAT Health Tech newsletter . You can reach Mario on Signal at mariojoze.13. Get email alerts for Mario Aguilar Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts As Google faces pressure to take greater accountability for the mental health impacts of its artificial intelligence products, the company’s clinical director Megan Jones Bell welcomed the challenge of making artificial intelligence helpful to people who come to its Gemini chatbot with a mental health crisis. “It can seem sometimes like shutting something down is a way of preventing harm,” Jones Bell told STAT. “We believe that making our product experience safer and more helpful and strengthening that bridge to support it is the more effective path to support mental health for the most people.” Advertisement Google recently made updates to its Gemini app so that it more prominently features connections to crisis hotlines when it detects a person may be at risk of self harm. In conversations about mental health, the AI will frequently point people to outside resources — but the bot doesn’t disengage, reminding a user, for example, that “I’m here to listen.” To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: Why do discussions about ‘brain health' ignore mental illness?
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion Why do discussions about ‘brain health’ ignore mental illness? The divide between mental illness and neurological disorders is a scientific and strategic error Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Husseini K. Manji , Eric J. Nestler ,  and Patrick J. Kennedy April 28, 2026 Manji is a professor at Oxford University, a professor (adjunct) at Yale University, and co-chair of the U.K. government Mental Health Goals program. Nestler is the Anne and Joel Ehrenkranz dean of the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, chief scientific officer and executive vice president of the Mount Sinai Health System, and a professor of neuroscience and psychiatry. Patrick Kennedy is a former U.S. congressman. Governments, industry, and philanthropies are investing in neuroscience at an unprecedented scale , and the ambition behind this impetus is a noble one: to reduce the growing burden of brain diseases and extend healthy cognitive life. We fully support this movement’s push for “brain health” to mirror successful frameworks established for cancer and heart health that prioritize early screening and aggressive preventive treatments, making it possible to act before irreversible damage sets in. Even as this agenda gains momentum, however, a critical blind spot is emerging. As governments refocus their policies to tackle conditions like Alzheimer’s disease and other neurodegenerative disorders, mental illness is often being sidelined as a secondary concern rather than as a primary component of brain health. This artificial divide is a scientific and a strategic error. Advertisement In many ways, the focus on Alzheimer’s disease is understandable. Worldwide, more than 55 million people live with Alzheimer’s and other dementias, a number that continues to grow with the rapid aging of the global population. This is undoubtedly a public health crisis. dementia , Mental Health , neuroscience , Policy Submit a correction request Reprints Husseini K. Manji Eric J. Nestler Patrick J. articles.title: A giant question looms over GOP's new Medicaid work requirements
articles.text:

STAT Plus Politics A giant question looms over GOP’s new Medicaid work requirements The law doesn’t require states to report on implementing work requirements. Will we know their consequences? Democrats say the law’s Medicaid work requirements will create red tape that leads to people losing their coverage . Republicans say the measure encourages work. Advertisement But because the law doesn’t require states to report on the implementation of this massive change to Medicaid, we may never get a detailed account of how the policy is working. articles.title: Trump administration warns against using federal dollars on fentanyl test strips
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Health Trump administration warns against using federal dollars on fentanyl test strips Key behavioral health agency also opposes medication-only addiction treatment Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Lev Facher April 27, 2026 Addiction Reporter Lev Facher lev.facher@statnews.com Lev Facher covers the U.S. addiction and overdose crisis. Get email alerts for Lev Facher Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts The Trump administration is doubling down on its opposition to harm reduction services for people who use illicit drugs. In an open letter on April 24, the federal agency overseeing addiction and mental health policy warned its grantees against using federal funds to buy harm reduction supplies including sterile syringes and pipes, or to distribute test strips for common drug supply adulterants like fentanyl, xylazine, and medetomidine. Advertisement In a second letter bearing the same date, the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration also warned against using certain addiction medications without accompanying support services. It wrote that medications including methadone and buprenorphine , which are shown to vastly reduce opioid overdose deaths, should be used as “part of the pathway to long-term recovery” but “not as a default sentence to life-long medication use.” addiction , HHS , opioids , public health Submit a correction request Reprints Lev Facher Addiction Reporter Lev Facher covers the U.S. addiction and overdose crisis. The Trump administration is doubling down on its opposition to harm reduction services for people who use illicit drugs. In an open letter on April 24, the federal agency overseeing addiction and mental health policy warned its grantees against using federal funds to buy harm reduction supplies including sterile syringes and pipes, or to distribute test strips for common drug supply adulterants like fentanyl, xylazine, and medetomidine. Advertisement In a second letter bearing the same date, the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration also warned against using certain addiction medications without accompanying support services. It wrote that medications including methadone and buprenorphine , which are shown to vastly reduce opioid overdose deaths, should be used as “part of the pathway to long-term recovery” but “not as a default sentence to life-long medication use.”

articles.title: Astellas retries XLMTM gene therapy after deaths
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Newsletter The Readout Astellas retries XLMTM gene therapy after deaths Veradermics’ hair loss pill late-stage trial success, FDA fast-tracks reviews for psychedelics, and more biotech news Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Meghana Keshavan April 27, 2026 Biotech Correspondent Meghana Keshavan meghana.keshavan@statnews.com Meghana Keshavan covers biotech and contributes to The Readout newsletter . Get email alerts for Meghana Keshavan Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Want to stay on top of the science and politics driving biotech today? Sign up to get our biotech newsletter in your inbox. New hair loss data for a sort of amped-up minoxidil is looking very plush. Also Astellas is reentering the clinic with a next-generation gene therapy for XLMTM and Intellia’s CRISPR therapy for hereditary angioedema impresses. Advertisement The need-to-know this morning Eli Lilly said it is buying Ajax Therapeutics , a privately held developer of blood cancer drugs, for as much as $2.3 billion. Ajax’s lead medicine is a next-generation JAK2 inhibitor currently being evaluated in a Phase 1 study in patients with myelofibrosis. Ligand Pharmaceuticals said it is buying Xoma , an aggregator of drug royalties, for $739 million. Compass Therapeutics said its drug for advanced biliary cancer delayed tumor progression but failed to prolong survival compared to chemotherapy in a clinical trial. BridgeBio shares were higher this morning on reports from the weekend that Pfizer had settled two of three patent cases regarding Vyndamax, its medicine for the heart condition known as ATTR-CM. While terms of the settlement have not been disclosed, any extension of patent protection for Vyndamax is also positive for BridgeBio, which sells a competing ATTR-CM drug called Attruby. CRISPR therapy slashes rare swelling attacks Intellia Therapeutics said this morning that its one-time CRISPR treatment lonvo-z cut attack rates by 87% in a Phase 3 trial of hereditary angioedema, STAT’s Jason Mast writes. With more than 60% of patients becoming attack-free and no serious safety signals reported, the therapy is being positioned as a functional cure — or at least a major upgrade over existing options. articles.title: Researchers Reflect on the Lasting Impact of Science’s Landmark Discoveries
articles.text:
Article Researchers Reflect on the Lasting Impact of Science’s Landmark Discoveries From PCR and CRISPR to autophagy and targeted cancer therapies, scientists share the discoveries that shaped their work and continue to influence the future of research. Written by The Scientist The Scientist View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . May 2, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Researchers share landmark discoveries that inspire them. Image credit: © iStock.com , Natalya Kosarevich PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share S cience is a series of milestones, from fundamental discoveries to innovative technologies. Each one acts like a building block, supporting the next breakthrough and paving the way for future generations of researchers. To explore the personal impact of these milestones, we asked our readers: Which landmark scientific discovery or invention do you find most inspiring, and why? Cell Biology: From Autophagy to Immortal Cells Kaamini Dhanabalan is a bioengineer and molecular imaging scientist. Kaamini Dhanabalan Kaamini Dhanabalan , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California, Santa Barbara I find the discovery of autophagy deeply inspiring. The idea that cells have an internal recycling system, one that breaks down damaged components and repurposes them for survival, completely changed how we think about health and aging. It shifted the narrative from cells passively accumulating damage to cells actively maintaining themselves. The significance of this work was recognized globally when the 2016 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for uncovering the mechanisms of autophagy, which underscored how a fundamental cellular process can reshape medicine. What fascinates me most is how central autophagy is across biological processes and diseases like neurodegeneration, cancer, immunity, metabolism, and aging. During my PhD, studying autophagy closely showed me how tightly regulated and context-dependent it is. Now seeing intermittent fasting enter mainstream conversations as a way to stimulate autophagy is striking. A molecular pathway once limited to research labs is now influencing everyday health decisions and remains my exact motivation to bridge benches and beds. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Microbiology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Khyati Raina’s work combines stem cell biology and organoid technology. Khyati Raina Khyati Raina , a postdoctoral fellow at Albert Einstein College of Medicine Two discoveries have really shaped how I think about science: the development of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing. The idea behind iPSCs is fascinating. It’s almost like reversing time at the cellular level. Scientists can take a fully developed cell, like a skin fibroblast, and reprogram it back into a pluripotent state by changing the set of proteins it expresses. From there, it has the potential to become almost any cell type in the body. The fact that you can generate cells so closely related to an individual, starting from their own tissue, completely changed how we study development and disease. CRISPR-Cas9, on the other hand, feels like having a highly precise molecular tool. It allows us to target and edit specific regions of DNA with remarkable accuracy. Together, these technologies have transformed research across many fields and continue to open up possibilities that once seemed out of reach. Molecular Biology: Amplifying and Silencing Genes Aditya Gupta is a parasitologist at USDA-ARS. Aditya Gupta Aditya Gupta , a postdoctoral fellow at the US Department of Agriculture-Agricultural Research Service (USDA-ARS) The invention of the polymerase chain reaction ( PCR ) by Kary Mullis is particularly inspiring to me. I read this story during my doctoral research, and I still remember how he ran back to his lab to check the PCR results. PCR transformed biology by allowing researchers to amplify tiny amounts of DNA and detect organisms that would otherwise remain hidden. In my work, PCR-based methods are essential for identifying parasites in meat samples and studying their genetic diversity. This technology illustrates how a single innovation can revolutionize multiple fields, from diagnostics to evolutionary biology, and enable discoveries that directly impact public health and disease surveillance. Kasturika Shankar is a biochemist and biophysicist who studies host-pathogen interactions. Kasturika Shankar Kasturika Shankar , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Buffalo The discovery of RNA interference, the finding that cells have a built-in system for silencing specific genes using small RNA molecules. Researchers stumbled upon it almost by accident, and yet it revolutionized both our understanding of gene regulation and gave scientists a powerful tool with which to study and potentially treat disease. It's a reminder that curiosity-driven, fundamental research can lead somewhere transformative in ways nobody initially anticipated. Health Science: From Precision Oncology to Harm Reduction Neil Carleton studies the tumor biology of age-related breast cancers. Neil Carleton Neil Carleton , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Pittsburgh The field of breast cancer research has been particularly inspiring to follow, both historically and in the modern era of precision oncology. The discovery of the estrogen receptor and the development of tamoxifen were among the earliest and most influential examples of targeted cancer therapy. These advances laid the groundwork for an entire field focused on endocrine-targeted treatments. Similarly, the identification of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) and the development of trastuzumab dramatically transformed outcomes for patients with HER2-positive breast cancer. Looking back at these milestones has been especially exciting, and they continue to inform how we think about precision medicine today. Mia Rough is a behavioral neuropharmacologist at Wake Forest University School of Medicine. Mia Rough Mia Rough , a postdoctoral fellow at Wake Forest University School of Medicine One of the developments I find most inspiring is the emergence of harm reduction tools such as naloxone (Narcan) and fentanyl test strips. What inspires me about these advances is that they reflect a public health approach grounded in compassion and evidence, where the immediate goal is to save lives. Harm reduction recognizes that people deserve care and protection regardless of where they are in recovery. To me, some of the most important advances in substance use science are the ones that reduce suffering in the present while also creating opportunities for better long-term outcomes. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage. Related Topics Cell & Molecular Biology , CRISPR , drug discovery , drugs , PCR , Public Health , technology Share You might also be interested in... Article Optimizing Cell Culture Workflows Getting cells to grow and thrive in culture involves finding the right conditions and developing the right protocols. Share Cell culture is a fundamental technique for life science applications ranging from research to industrial manufacturing. Finding the optimal conditions for specific cell types to grow and thrive is a difficult task that scientists routinely face. Researchers must also contend with external obstacles such as contamination, workflow inefficiencies, and the logistics of scaling. Download this compendium to learn more about tackling cell culture challenges big and small, from learning about bioprocessing and 3D cell culture to fine-tuning established protocols. Here is a look at what’s inside Article: An Introduction to Bioprocessing Article: Understanding the 3D Cell Culture Revolution White Paper: Solving Common Challenges of Mammalian Cell Shake Flask Culture White Paper: Preventing Contamination in CO 2 Incubator Shakers Sponsored by Top Image Credit: ©iStock, Tobias Buschmann You might also be interested in... The Nutshell Donated Mitochondria Help Alleviate Nerve Damage and Pain in Mice Transferring mitochondria, or the glial cells that produce them, to mice with nerve damage mitigated pain, suggesting a promising path to treat neuropathic pain. Written by Andrea Lius, PhD Andrea Lius, PhD Andrea Lius is a freelance science writer. She is working on her PhD in pharmacology at the University of Washington in Seattle. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jan 7, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later Mitochondrial transfer from glial cells to neighboring sensory neurons reduced neuropathic pain in mice, indicating its therapeutic potential. Image credit: ©iStock.com, Chinnapong PDF version Share F ast-acting cells, such as sensory neurons, require a lot of energy to function, so they have a high demand for mitochondria. But how do these cells generate and maintain enough of these powerhouse organelles to sustain themselves? Ru-Rong Ji , a pain researcher at Duke University, and his colleagues recently discovered that glial cells that surround sensory neurons play a critical role in this process: They transfer mitochondria to their neighbors. 1 When the researchers blocked this process in mice, the animals experienced more nerve damage and pain. These findings, published in Nature , highlight mitochondrial transfer as a possible solution for treating chronic pain in humans. Ru-Rong Ji, a researcher at Duke University, studies the mechanisms of chronic pain. Duke University School of Medicine/Jack Newman Researchers once thought that each cell is responsible for its own mitochondria supply, but mounting evidence suggests that the organelles can move between neighboring cells . Ji and his colleagues wondered if this was also the case for sensory neurons and their surrounding glial cells. To address this question, the researchers co-cultured mice sensory neurons and glial cells and labeled their mitochondria with a dye (MitoTracker). They watched the two cell types interact under the microscope and observed that over 80 percent of neurons received mitochondria from glial cells via a few different mechanisms. Blocking endocytosis, the formation of gap junctions, and specialized structures called tunneling nanotubes (TNT) suppressed mitochondrial transfer. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Next, Ji and his colleagues investigated if their findings in cultured cells held true in live animals. They labeled the mitochondria of mice glial cells, and then they imaged the cells and their neighboring sensory neurons five and 10 days later. On the tenth day, 23 percent of neurons contained mitochondria from glial cells. Inhibiting endocytosis and TNT formation also significantly decreased mitochondrial transfer from glial cells to sensory neurons, aligning with the researchers’ in vitro data. To further evaluate how applicable their results were to humans, Ji’s team analyzed human neurons and glial cells from healthy and diabetic donors (up to 50 percent of patients with diabetes experience nerve damage). There was less mitochondrial transfer between the glial cells and sensory neurons of patients with diabetes than their healthy counterparts. Furthermore, the introduction of healthy human donors’ glial cells—or mitochondria isolated from these cells—to diabetic mice alleviated their hypersensitivity to touch, a key symptom of neuropathic pain. “By giving damaged nerves fresh mitochondria—or helping them make more of their own—we can reduce inflammation and support healing,” said Ji in a statement . “This approach has the potential to ease pain in a completely new way.” Reference Xu J, et al. Mitochondrial transfer from glia to neurons protects peripheral neuropathy . 2025. Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage. Related Topics Cell & Molecular Biology , diabetes , mitochondria , nerve damage , neuropathy , Neuroscience , pain Meet the Author Andrea Lius, PhD Andrea Lius is an intern at The Scientist. She earned her PhD in pharmacology from the University of Washington. Besides science, she also enjoys writing short-form creative nonfiction. Validation and Analysis of 12,000 AI-Driven CAR T Designs in the Bits to Binders Competition Examine how large-scale CAR T-cell functional validation informs the efficacy of AI-driven protein binder design methodologies. Designing Translational CRISPR Genome Editing A platform approach to gene editing brings together design, manufacturing, validation, analytics, and regulatory considerations right from the start. Automating Large-Scale Plasmid DNA Purification In this webinar, Lee Hoang will examine automated strategies for improving scalability and consistency in large-scale plasmid DNA purification. Products Product News Thermo Scientific™ Multiskan™ Ease Absorbance Microplate Reader Absorbance, redefined. Multiskan™ Ease delivers fast, reproducible results with minimal training. LED optics remove lamp changes, while cloud-enabled SkanIt™ software simplifies setup and analysis for reliable ELISA workflows. 3dGRO Human iPSC Derived Liver Organoid Progenitors The 3dGRO Human iPSC Derived Liver Organoid Progenitor express key liver progenitor markers HNF4a, SOX17, FOXA2 and SOX9, and showed proper apical and basal-lateral polarization marked by E-cadherin and ZO1 expression. Gibco CTS Compleo Fill and Finish System – Automated Formulation and Filling to Help Streamline Cell Therapy Manufacturing The CTS Compleo system combines a closed, automated design with intuitive software to streamline formulation and filling while maintaining aseptic integrity and helping reduce risk in cell therapy manufacturing workflows. New CellCelector CLD takes you from thousands of candidates to the top clone, faster Rapid preselection of high-producing clones from candidate pools, saving resources We’ve updated our Privacy Policy to make it clearer how we use your personal data. Please read our Cookie Policy to learn how we use cookies to provide you with a better experience. Article Researchers Reflect on the Lasting Impact of Science’s Landmark Discoveries From PCR and CRISPR to autophagy and targeted cancer therapies, scientists share the discoveries that shaped their work and continue to influence the future of research. Written by The Scientist The Scientist View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . May 2, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Researchers share landmark discoveries that inspire them. Image credit: © iStock.com , Natalya Kosarevich PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share S cience is a series of milestones, from fundamental discoveries to innovative technologies. Each one acts like a building block, supporting the next breakthrough and paving the way for future generations of researchers. To explore the personal impact of these milestones, we asked our readers: Which landmark scientific discovery or invention do you find most inspiring, and why? Cell Biology: From Autophagy to Immortal Cells Kaamini Dhanabalan is a bioengineer and molecular imaging scientist. Kaamini Dhanabalan Kaamini Dhanabalan , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California, Santa Barbara I find the discovery of autophagy deeply inspiring. The idea that cells have an internal recycling system, one that breaks down damaged components and repurposes them for survival, completely changed how we think about health and aging. It shifted the narrative from cells passively accumulating damage to cells actively maintaining themselves. The significance of this work was recognized globally when the 2016 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for uncovering the mechanisms of autophagy, which underscored how a fundamental cellular process can reshape medicine. What fascinates me most is how central autophagy is across biological processes and diseases like neurodegeneration, cancer, immunity, metabolism, and aging. During my PhD, studying autophagy closely showed me how tightly regulated and context-dependent it is. Now seeing intermittent fasting enter mainstream conversations as a way to stimulate autophagy is striking. A molecular pathway once limited to research labs is now influencing everyday health decisions and remains my exact motivation to bridge benches and beds. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Microbiology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Khyati Raina’s work combines stem cell biology and organoid technology. Khyati Raina Khyati Raina , a postdoctoral fellow at Albert Einstein College of Medicine Two discoveries have really shaped how I think about science: the development of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing. The idea behind iPSCs is fascinating. It’s almost like reversing time at the cellular level. Scientists can take a fully developed cell, like a skin fibroblast, and reprogram it back into a pluripotent state by changing the set of proteins it expresses. From there, it has the potential to become almost any cell type in the body. The fact that you can generate cells so closely related to an individual, starting from their own tissue, completely changed how we study development and disease. CRISPR-Cas9, on the other hand, feels like having a highly precise molecular tool. It allows us to target and edit specific regions of DNA with remarkable accuracy. Together, these technologies have transformed research across many fields and continue to open up possibilities that once seemed out of reach. Molecular Biology: Amplifying and Silencing Genes Aditya Gupta is a parasitologist at USDA-ARS. Aditya Gupta Aditya Gupta , a postdoctoral fellow at the US Department of Agriculture-Agricultural Research Service (USDA-ARS) The invention of the polymerase chain reaction ( PCR ) by Kary Mullis is particularly inspiring to me. I read this story during my doctoral research, and I still remember how he ran back to his lab to check the PCR results. PCR transformed biology by allowing researchers to amplify tiny amounts of DNA and detect organisms that would otherwise remain hidden. In my work, PCR-based methods are essential for identifying parasites in meat samples and studying their genetic diversity. This technology illustrates how a single innovation can revolutionize multiple fields, from diagnostics to evolutionary biology, and enable discoveries that directly impact public health and disease surveillance. Kasturika Shankar is a biochemist and biophysicist who studies host-pathogen interactions. Kasturika Shankar Kasturika Shankar , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Buffalo The discovery of RNA interference, the finding that cells have a built-in system for silencing specific genes using small RNA molecules. Researchers stumbled upon it almost by accident, and yet it revolutionized both our understanding of gene regulation and gave scientists a powerful tool with which to study and potentially treat disease. It's a reminder that curiosity-driven, fundamental research can lead somewhere transformative in ways nobody initially anticipated. Health Science: From Precision Oncology to Harm Reduction Neil Carleton studies the tumor biology of age-related breast cancers. Neil Carleton Neil Carleton , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Pittsburgh The field of breast cancer research has been particularly inspiring to follow, both historically and in the modern era of precision oncology. The discovery of the estrogen receptor and the development of tamoxifen were among the earliest and most influential examples of targeted cancer therapy. These advances laid the groundwork for an entire field focused on endocrine-targeted treatments. Similarly, the identification of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) and the development of trastuzumab dramatically transformed outcomes for patients with HER2-positive breast cancer. Looking back at these milestones has been especially exciting, and they continue to inform how we think about precision medicine today. Mia Rough is a behavioral neuropharmacologist at Wake Forest University School of Medicine. Mia Rough Mia Rough , a postdoctoral fellow at Wake Forest University School of Medicine One of the developments I find most inspiring is the emergence of harm reduction tools such as naloxone (Narcan) and fentanyl test strips. What inspires me about these advances is that they reflect a public health approach grounded in compassion and evidence, where the immediate goal is to save lives. Harm reduction recognizes that people deserve care and protection regardless of where they are in recovery. To me, some of the most important advances in substance use science are the ones that reduce suffering in the present while also creating opportunities for better long-term outcomes. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage. The Nutshell Donated Mitochondria Help Alleviate Nerve Damage and Pain in Mice Transferring mitochondria, or the glial cells that produce them, to mice with nerve damage mitigated pain, suggesting a promising path to treat neuropathic pain. Written by Andrea Lius, PhD Andrea Lius, PhD Andrea Lius is a freelance science writer. She is working on her PhD in pharmacology at the University of Washington in Seattle. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jan 7, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later Mitochondrial transfer from glial cells to neighboring sensory neurons reduced neuropathic pain in mice, indicating its therapeutic potential. Image credit: ©iStock.com, Chinnapong PDF version Share F ast-acting cells, such as sensory neurons, require a lot of energy to function, so they have a high demand for mitochondria. But how do these cells generate and maintain enough of these powerhouse organelles to sustain themselves? Ru-Rong Ji , a pain researcher at Duke University, and his colleagues recently discovered that glial cells that surround sensory neurons play a critical role in this process: They transfer mitochondria to their neighbors. 1 When the researchers blocked this process in mice, the animals experienced more nerve damage and pain. These findings, published in Nature , highlight mitochondrial transfer as a possible solution for treating chronic pain in humans. Ru-Rong Ji, a researcher at Duke University, studies the mechanisms of chronic pain. Duke University School of Medicine/Jack Newman Researchers once thought that each cell is responsible for its own mitochondria supply, but mounting evidence suggests that the organelles can move between neighboring cells . Ji and his colleagues wondered if this was also the case for sensory neurons and their surrounding glial cells. To address this question, the researchers co-cultured mice sensory neurons and glial cells and labeled their mitochondria with a dye (MitoTracker). They watched the two cell types interact under the microscope and observed that over 80 percent of neurons received mitochondria from glial cells via a few different mechanisms. Blocking endocytosis, the formation of gap junctions, and specialized structures called tunneling nanotubes (TNT) suppressed mitochondrial transfer. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Next, Ji and his colleagues investigated if their findings in cultured cells held true in live animals. They labeled the mitochondria of mice glial cells, and then they imaged the cells and their neighboring sensory neurons five and 10 days later. On the tenth day, 23 percent of neurons contained mitochondria from glial cells. Inhibiting endocytosis and TNT formation also significantly decreased mitochondrial transfer from glial cells to sensory neurons, aligning with the researchers’ in vitro data. To further evaluate how applicable their results were to humans, Ji’s team analyzed human neurons and glial cells from healthy and diabetic donors (up to 50 percent of patients with diabetes experience nerve damage). There was less mitochondrial transfer between the glial cells and sensory neurons of patients with diabetes than their healthy counterparts. Furthermore, the introduction of healthy human donors’ glial cells—or mitochondria isolated from these cells—to diabetic mice alleviated their hypersensitivity to touch, a key symptom of neuropathic pain. “By giving damaged nerves fresh mitochondria—or helping them make more of their own—we can reduce inflammation and support healing,” said Ji in a statement . “This approach has the potential to ease pain in a completely new way.” Reference Xu J, et al. Mitochondrial transfer from glia to neurons protects peripheral neuropathy . 2025. Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage.
